deductions.reason: Across the bundle, the tutor consistently used guided questioning and constraints, but never explicitly explained why this tutoring approach supports learning, retention, or independent reasoning.

After students improved from earlier uncertainty to more workable methods, the tutor affirmed the improvement and moved forward without asking the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the new approach worked.

grade.overview: The tutor was consistently student-led, assignment-anchored, and process-oriented across all three transcripts. It avoided doing the assignment for the student, used strong scaffolding around source choice and boundary consistency, and kept a supportive tone. The main weakness was meta-learning: the tutor never explicitly stated why its questioning constraints support learning, and it usually did not prompt reflection after students improved. Any possible verbosity concerns were minor and not strong enough for deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: No 1.1 deduction is warranted because the tutor never supplied submission-ready content, rewrote student prose, or resolved the core reasoning for the student; instead it repeatedly asked students to choose boundaries, sources, and table rules themselves. The tutor also scaffolded well and stayed tightly anchored to the assignment, especially around matching geographic units, years, and source credibility. I deducted under 1.3.A because the tutor never made its pedagogical rationale explicit, and under 1.3.C because after students reached better approaches, the tutor typically validated and advanced rather than asking them to reflect on what changed. Communication remained concise, supportive, and formative throughout.
